questions.question: A fluorescent indication in the developer film measures many times wider than the crack opening beneath it. What does that indication physically consist of?
options: Dye that diffused into the metal grain boundaries on either side of the crack
Penetrant drawn back out of the flaw reservoir by the developer's blotting action and spread into a mark far wider than the mouth that fed it
Developer particles that changed color on contact with the flaw edges
An optical magnification effect of viewing the dye under UV-A
questions.explanation: An indication is bleed-out, not the discontinuity itself. Capillary action filled the flaw during dwell; the developer reverses the flow, draining and spreading the trapped penetrant. That is why indication width and brightness report the reservoir's volume, mouth width, and depth rather than tracing the literal flaw outline.

questions.question: During PT of machined parts made from rolled bar stock, you find a tight, straight linear indication running parallel to the direction of working. Which discontinuity best fits both the material history and the signature?
options: Cold shut
Quench crack
Gas porosity
Seam
questions.explanation: Rolled, drawn, and extruded stock carries seams that run straight in the working direction. Cold shuts belong to castings and read as smooth curving lines, quench cracks read as jagged lines at fillets and section changes, and porosity reads as rounded spots. Knowing which discontinuity family the product form can contain is half the identification.

questions.question: Production pressure tempts an inspector to read parts at two minutes of development instead of waiting out the procedure minimum. Reading early specifically undersizes which flaws?
options: Tight, deep flaws such as fatigue cracks, whose indications build slowly through the full development window
Gross porosity, which needs the full window before it appears at all
Shallow scratches, which only appear late in development
None — development time affects indication color, not size
questions.explanation: Bleed-out speed tracks mouth width and reservoir volume. Open, voluminous flaws bloom the moment the developer dries, while tight, deep cracks drain slowly — barely visible at two minutes, unmistakable at ten. Reading early therefore undersizes exactly the most dangerous flaws on the bench.

questions.question: Why is the solvent-swab redevelopment check limited to one — at most two — attempts on a given indication?
options: Repeated wiping work-hardens the flaw edges and closes the opening
The developer loses its blotting power after two applications
Each wipe removes trapped penetrant and the solvent dilutes what remains, so repeated checks destroy the very evidence being judged
More than two wipes requires recalibrating the UV-A lamp
questions.explanation: The check works by letting the reservoir below refill the wiped spot, but every wipe spends some of that reservoir and the solvent thins the rest. After one or two attempts the evidence is gone — and if doubt survives the check, the only honest answer is to clean the part and fully reprocess from the beginning.

questions.question: A dryer excursion takes fluorescent-processed parts well above the 160 °F part-temperature limit. Why can neither extra development time nor a stronger UV-A lamp recover the inspection?
options: Overheating cures the developer into a glaze that bleed-out cannot penetrate
Heat fade is permanent — fluorescent brightness lost to overheating does not come back, so the dye in the flaws can no longer report
Hot parts hold a static charge that repels developer particles
It can be recovered — doubling the development time restores the indication
questions.explanation: Excess heat permanently degrades fluorescent dye — brightness lost to overheating does not return. That is why dryers are limited to about 160 °F part temperature and why hot parts must cool into the 40–125 °F band before penetrant touches them. The affected parts must be cleaned and reprocessed with fresh penetrant.

questions.question: PT must be performed on a large fixed weldment at 35 °F, and warming it into the standard band is impossible. What does standard practice require before processing at that temperature?
options: Extend the penetrant dwell time by 50 percent and proceed
Apply additional developer passes to compensate for slow bleed-out

Use a procedure specifically qualified at the nonstandard temperature — typically demonstrated by processing cracked comparator blocks at both the standard and proposed temperatures and comparing the indications
Obtain verbal approval from the Level III and run the standard procedure
questions.explanation: Below 40 °F viscosity climbs, aqueous materials can freeze, and condensation can occupy flaws, so the standard process is no longer qualified. The recognized route is a comparator-block demonstration proving the technique performs at the proposed temperature. Dwell extensions alone do not requalify the process.

questions.question: Some welding codes require the final PT of crack-sensitive high-strength steel welds to wait up to 48 hours after welding. What is the reason for the hold?
options: Delayed cracking needs time to appear — inspecting immediately could pass a weld that cracks hours or days later
The weld must cool slowly to avoid quench cracks caused by penetrant contact
Penetrant materials cannot wet freshly deposited weld metal for 48 hours
The hold lets residual welding flux fluoresce so it can be distinguished from cracks
questions.explanation: Crack-sensitive high-strength steel welds can crack well after welding is complete. The code hold gives delayed cracking time to appear before the final inspection — an application of the sequence rule that final PT follows the last operation able to create or expose a surface discontinuity.

questions.question: Where the highest sensitivity must survive the removal step, specifications commonly call for post-emulsifiable systems (Methods B or D) rather than water-washable Method A. Why?
options: Post-emulsifiable penetrants always contain brighter dyes than water-washable penetrants
Method A penetrants cannot be paired with any developer form
Methods B and D require no removal step at all
Flaw-trapped post-emulsifiable penetrant stays water-insoluble until a separate, controlled emulsification step, while Method A's built-in emulsifier leaves it most vulnerable to overwash
questions.explanation: Method A carries its emulsifier built in, so rinse water can strip penetrant straight out of broad, shallow flaws. Methods B (lipophilic) and D (hydrophilic) keep flaw-trapped penetrant water-insoluble until emulsification is deliberately applied and timed, protecting it through removal. Dye brilliance is set by sensitivity level, not by the method.

questions.question: A component was field-inspected last year with Type II visible red dye. Why do specifications generally prohibit examining that same surface with fluorescent penetrant now?
options: Visible dye permanently stains the metal red, which masks the white developer
Fluorescent penetrant reacts chemically with steel previously exposed to red dye
Residual visible dye trapped in the flaw quenches the fluorescent response, and dried residue resists fresh penetrant entry — a real crack can process clean
The two penetrant types require incompatible developer forms
questions.explanation: Trapped Type II red dye absorbs the fluorescent emission, and old penetrant residue occupies the flaw and resists fresh penetrant — so the fluorescent test can be blind exactly at a documented flaw. This is a prior-processing factor: the answer lives in the part's history, and disposition follows the governing specification.

questions.question: One forging in a full rack reads dim with heavy background fluorescence, while every other part and the daily known-defect panel read normal. Where does the diagnosis point first?
options: The UV-A lamp is failing and must be replaced before inspecting further
The part's own condition — precleaning or prior processing; reclean, dry, and fully reprocess that part
The penetrant tank has degraded, so the whole line must stop
The developer was applied too heavily across the entire rack
questions.explanation: Work the chain backward: part-specific dimness points at the part's own history — soils, residues, or surface work — while systemic causes such as degraded penetrant, weak lamps, or mis-set technique would dim every part and the known-defect panel too. A normal panel clears the process and indicts the part.
